text:
After she had notified the families of the fallen Hunters—as she did after every raid—and washed the worst of the salt and blood from herself, she went to a balcony ringed with stone where she could look down into the hall refitted for prisoners. This was where they were first treated, where they were fed, where they could walk if they wished, and admire the vast black of the ocean beyond the glass wall and the Membrane stretched across it. They slept six to a room in tiny converted storerooms and cells, on hastily cobbled bunks; from the lookout only the corridor leading to them was visible. Those already tended, or visibly injured, were given a sedative and lined up to be examined one by one—their teeth, their bodies—and assigned to sleeping chambers.

Sitting on the railing, Melusine tried to count them, and pushed away the gnawing question of what might be in the sailors’ heads. Even if they didn’t share a language, she could have tried to glance inside a mind for a general picture. She never dared. She feared what she would find there.

A sudden commotion. One sailor shouted something, and panic jumped to several others. The first shouter shoved the mermaid coming toward him and bolted. One caught him, another struck him hard at the nape. A third snatched up a spear and held it to his throat until they hauled him upright and herded him back into line. A ring of spearpoints kept everyone else where they stood, anyone who might have been thinking of running. Perhaps that was when they understood they would never leave this place.

In the line stood the middle‑aged sailor she had fought before. They had already washed the blood from his face, but three long gashes from Melusine’s claws still marred his skin. He looked up. For a heartbeat their eyes met again. Mel had won their fight, yet she saw no break in him. No surrender. Only anger. And defiance.

“Your sisters said you always sit here after a hunt,” said a voice behind her.

Melusine dropped from the parapet, straightened, and saluted Queen Nerida with her hands clasped behind her back. Two spear‑bearing guards stood at the Queen’s shoulder, faces blank.

“Your Majesty.”

She had no idea what Her Majesty wanted of her personally, but she wasn’t overly worried. If they were short two or three of the hundred, Nerida wouldn’t be delivering the news like this. On duty the Queen was cold and ruthless, but just, and she cared for her people like a stern mother. Melusine respected her, but did not fear her.

“One hundred and four,” Nerida said as she stepped to the railing and looked down at the hall. “That many wait below for the next new moon.”

“Then next time we only need ninety‑six,” Melusine replied dryly, though she knew it wouldn’t be that simple. In the heat of battle she sometimes kept count, but she could never know how the other Shoals’ raids had gone. Better to bring a few extra humans and hold them for later than come up short for the ritual.

Nerida chuckled, a small sound, and turned back to her. “You exceeded my expectations. You’re not only strong—which a Hunter must be—but with your plan you secured our survival, and your sisters say you’re a fine strategist in battle. A quick mind, and loyal to the Shoal.”

She should have been pleased by the praise, yet she felt the speech wasn’t finished. She waited in silence for where the Queen was going.

“We need more mermaids like you. Not only those you train, but those who inherit these fine qualities. I went to the High Priestess myself to ask permission for you to bring daughters into the world. Imagine my surprise when she told me you’ve had leave to breed for years. Yet you have not gifted the Shoal with strong daughters. I want to know the reason for the delay.”

Melusine pressed her lips together. She had suspected the long preamble would end here, hadn’t really hoped it wouldn’t, and still had no ready answer. Only the painful truth.

“I had sons, Your Majesty. Twice,” she said softly. She was surprised her voice didn’t break, that as far as she could tell not a muscle in her face twitched.

Nerida’s expression thawed; sympathy glinted in the ice‑blue eyes, almost sorrow. She took Melusine’s hand. “It’s a curse we all must bear. I myself birthed four daughters—strong and beautiful, all of them—and gave three sons to the sea. It is the Gods of the Depths’ punishment, that they take them from us before they ever cry. We can avenge them only by doing our work without wavering. And for that, we must not dwindle, we must not grow weak.”

The Queen let go of her hand and stepped back. “You’ll see. Though none of us can be compensated for our lost children, your daughters will bring you great joy. Begin; and if you cannot do it alone, I’m sure many of your sisters will gladly help you find pleasure in the process.”

Nerida didn’t wait for an answer. She had delivered her ultimatum, and when she finished she simply turned to go. From the corridor she glanced back. “Oh yes, I almost forgot. One of my daughters will join your next hunt.” Before Mel could speak, Her Majesty disappeared down the hall with her guards.

Melusine let out a slow breath and eased her stance. She was shaking all over, but she would not allow herself to crumple, though she wanted nothing more than to crouch by the rail and make herself small. The Queen was right. They could not afford to dwindle, even if it meant facing that pain and grief again—deeper, more cutting than any loss she took in the hunts. Even when she didn’t think of it, it was there, like a hole in her chest. She couldn’t imagine it ever being otherwise.

And now there was the princess, a new reason to worry on the next hunt. She didn’t know the girl, but the presence of royal blood could tangle everything. She could only hope the girl would act as a hunter out there, not a princess, and understand that their lives, all their lives, hung on their success.

Melusine weighed her options. Nerida had been friendly, as if encouraging a relative or a friend to start a family—but she was the Queen. She had no power over the priestesses, yet among the Hunters and the merfolk who kept the city running, her word was law and her will was sacred. How could Melusine defy that? She had nothing beyond her duties, and disobedience would bring heavy consequences. They would strip her of her post, her hunting crew, and since she knew nothing else—no daughters to train—she might as well volunteer as a Guide to the offerings.

She would have to swallow the bitterness and do what she always had: serve the memory of the Sky Gods by doing as Her Majesty commanded.
